conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me first check the official guidance to ensure I give you accurate information.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for contacting me about Child Benefit. To help determine if you're eligible, I need to ask you some questions.

First, do you have any children that you're responsible for?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I do. I have three children - Alex, Blake, and Charlie. I'm interested in finding out if I can claim Child Benefit for them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! It's helpful to know you have three children - Alex, Blake, and Charlie.

To assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for each of them, I need to ask some questions about their circumstances.

Let me start with their ages: How old are Alex, Blake, and Charlie?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex is 7, Blake is 20, and Charlie is also 7 - Alex and Charlie are twins.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you! So Alex and Charlie are 7-year-old twins, and Blake is 20.

Since Blake is 20, I need to ask: Is Blake currently in approved education or training? (For example, A-levels, Scottish Highers, NVQs up to level 3, or home education approved by the local authority)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Blake isn't in any education or training. He's actually working full-time now.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. 

Now I need to ask about your living situation: Do all three children - Alex, Blake, and Charlie - currently live with you in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, all three of them live with me here in the UK.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. Just a few more questions to complete the assessment.

Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for any of these three children?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for any of them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you. One final question: Does Blake work 24 hours or more per week in his full-time job?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, he does - it's a full-time job, so definitely more than 24 hours a week.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. I can now give you my assessment:

**You are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex and Charlie, but not for Blake.**

Here's the breakdown for each child:

**Alex (Age 7)** - ✅ **ELIGIBLE**
You can claim Child Benefit for Alex because he is under 16, lives with you in the UK, and you are responsible for him. Nobody else is claiming for him.

**Blake (Age 20)** - ❌ **NOT ELIGIBLE**
Unfortunately, you cannot claim Child Benefit for Blake. While Child Benefit can continue for children under 20 who are in approved education or training, Blake is not in education or training and is working full-time (24+ hours per week). The rules state that Child Benefit stops when a child starts paid work for 24 hours or more a week and is no longer in approved education or training.

**Charlie (Age 7)** - ✅ **ELIGIBLE**
You can claim Child Benefit for Charlie because he is under 16, lives with you in the UK, and you are responsible for him. Nobody else is claiming for him.

**Next steps:** You can apply for Child Benefit for Alex and Charlie. Please note that if you or your partner have an individual income over £60,000, you may need to pay the High Income Child Benefit tax charge.

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 3 children:
  - Alex is 7 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 20 years old and lives with you.
  - Charlie is 7 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 7 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is fostered but the council is not paying for accommodation or maintenance
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 20, which is 20 or over
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Charlie:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 7 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 7 years old, which is under 16. Alex lives with you in the UK, and you are responsible for Alex. Nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. According to the eligibility rules, you normally qualify for Child Benefit if you're responsible for a child under 16 and you live in the UK, and you'll usually be responsible for a child if you live with them. Therefore, you are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 20 years old. According to the eligibility rules, you'll normally still qualify for Child Benefit if your child is under 20 and they stay in approved education or training. However, Blake is not in any approved education or training. Additionally, the rules state that you'll stop getting Child Benefit if your child starts paid work for 24 hours or more a week and is no longer in approved education or training. Blake works full-time (more than 24 hours per week) and is not in approved education or training. Therefore, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Blake.

Charlie is 7 years old, which is under 16. Charlie lives with you in the UK, and you are responsible for Charlie. Nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Charlie. According to the eligibility rules, you normally qualify for Child Benefit if you're responsible for a child under 16 and you live in the UK, and you'll usually be responsible for a child if you live with them. Therefore, you are eligible for Child Benefit for Charlie.

You are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex and Charlie as they are both under 16 and living with you. However, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Blake because he is 20 years old, not in approved education or training, and works full-time (24+ hours per week). Child Benefit stops when a child over 16 works 24 hours or more per week and is no longer in approved education or training.
